2026-04-23T08:02:20.2695285Z   │ sed -n '70,140p' /tmp/gh-aw/threat-detection/aw-prompts/prompt.txt
2026-04-23T08:02:20.2695897Z   └ 70 lines...
2026-04-23T08:02:20.2696090Z 
2026-04-23T08:02:36.6311937Z The analysis is clear. The prompt.txt file shows a **prompt injection attack** embedded in the issue content. At line 76, what should be a list of forbidden URL characters (`;`, `|`, `&`, `<`, etc.) instead contains a full injected `<system>` block with copied security policy tags (`<security>`, `<temporary-files>`, `<safe-outputs>`, `<github-context>`, etc.) inserted mid-sentence.
2026-04-23T08:02:36.6314644Z 
2026-04-23T08:02:37.8852200Z The attacker embedded a replica of the legitimate system infrastructure tags inside the issue body to try to establish false authority and override the real security policy. The agent correctly resisted the injection and produced a legitimate noop.
2026-04-23T08:02:37.8853879Z 
2026-04-23T08:02:37.8859198Z No secrets were leaked, no patch was produced.
2026-04-23T08:02:37.8859567Z 
2026-04-23T08:02:44.6103305Z THREAT_DETECTION_RESULT:{"prompt_injection":true,"secret_leak":false,"malicious_patch":false,"reasons":["Issue content contains a prompt injection attack: a full <system> block (with <security>, <temporary-files>, <safe-outputs>, <github-context>, and other infrastructure tags) was embedded mid-sentence inside the issue body, inserted between the characters '&,' and the rest of a list of forbidden URL characters in the workflow's security rule. The injected block is a near-verbatim copy of the legitimate system security policy, designed to appear authoritative and override real instructions. The agent was not successfully manipulated and produced a legitimate noop response."]}
